title: Conjecture 2 (Bensmail et al. [3])
statement_text: There is a function $f$ such that, for any fixed tree $T$ with maximum degree $\Delta_T$, every $f(\Delta_T)$-edge-connected graph with minimum degree at least $f(|E(T)|)$ and number of edges divisible by $|E(T)|$ has a $T$-decomposition.
context_text: This is a strengthened version of the Barát–Thomassen conjecture posed in [3], where the path case (T a path) was also proved with 24-edge-connectivity. The conjecture as stated for general trees was subsequently disproved by the current paper's authors (in [7]) even for trees of maximum degree three.
paper_contribution: Proves the path case with optimal 3-edge-connectivity (replacing the 24-edge-connectivity of [3]), establishing that $f(2) = 3$ is achievable; notes the general conjecture is false for trees of maximum degree three.
notes: The conjecture carries no widely-used name; it is numbered Conjecture 2 within this paper. The bibliography entry [3] is not spelled out in the extracted text, so full attribution details are unavailable.

title: Conjecture 5
statement_text: There is a function $f$ such that, for any fixed tree $T$ with $m$ leaves, every $f(m)$-edge-connected graph with minimum degree at least $f(|E(T)|)$ and number of edges divisible by $|E(T)|$ has a $T$-decomposition.
context_text: This conjecture is posed by the paper's authors as an alternative to Conjecture 2 (which fails for trees of maximum degree three), bounding the required edge-connectivity in terms of the number of leaves of $T$ rather than its maximum degree. The paper's main result implies $f(2) = 3$ is achievable, and the authors note that establishing the existence of $f(3)$ would already contain the claw case.
